summary: Gruber argues that Apple's first year-over-year quarterly sales decline since 2003, while not good news, has been met with a hysterical and disproportionate response from investors and media. He notes that Apple's actual results were nearly in line with its own guidance and that the company still earned more profit than Alphabet, Facebook, and Microsoft combined. Drawing on Dr. Drang's analysis comparing the iPhone 6's exceptional sales to an El Niño spike, Gruber suggests the decline may be a statistical blip rather than evidence of 'peak iPhone.' He presents Q2 iPhone sales data showing the iPhone 6 was the anomaly, not the iPhone 6S decline.
key_points: Apple's results were within its own guidance — the stock's 6.25% drop ($40 billion in market cap) was an overreaction to information that should not have been surprising.
Despite the 'bad' quarter, Apple's $10.5 billion profit exceeded the combined earnings of Alphabet, Facebook, and Microsoft, illustrating the absurd altitude Apple operates at.
Dr. Drang's El Niño analogy is apt: the iPhone 6 was an anomalous sales spike, and judging the 6S against it creates a misleading picture of decline.
There is insufficient data to declare 'peak iPhone' — the Q2 sales chart shows the iPhone 6 as the outlier, with other years forming a roughly linear growth trend.
Gruber compares investors and media to children who fail the marshmallow test: incapable of looking past the next few months.
money_quotes: Certainly not enough to justify today's 6.25 percent drop in the company's stock price, which knocked about $40 billion off the company's market cap. That's more than the value of Netflix. Welcome to the casino.

That's what happens after an almost unfathomable streak of over 50 consecutive quarters of year-over-year growth — you reach an altitude at which even an indisputably very bad quarter still leaves you with enormous, industry-leading profits.
A concerning quarter? Yes. Surprising? Shouldn't have been. Alarming? Not even close.
I think an awful lot of investors and members of the news media are like those kids who couldn't wait: no ability whatsoever to look past the next few months.
The iPhone 6 was Apple's El Niño.
There is no product in the world that generates 'opening weekend' sales like the iPhone.
Apple's $10.5 billion in net income for the last three months was more than the yearly profits of all but 15 companies in the S&P 500 index.
key_insight: Apple's first sales decline in 13 years looks alarming only when measured against the anomalous iPhone 6 spike, and the market's panicked reaction reveals a fundamental inability to evaluate long-term trends.
